topic: sample clearance headache — dancehall sample in a track

turns.content: I am STRESSED right now. so I used a dancehall sample in a track and now I gotta figure out the whole copyright clearance ting and I have NO idea where to even start?? like do I need a lawyer for this??

Deep breath — sample clearance is stressful but it's doable. Let me help you figure out where you stand. A few things I need to know:

1. What did you sample exactly — a melody, vocals, a drum break, a riddim?
2. Do you know who owns the original recording? (Label, artist, etc.)
3. Is this track already released or still unreleased?
4. How much of the original did you use — a few seconds, a loop throughout the track?

its a vocal chop from a 90s dancehall track. like 2 seconds of a vocal that I pitched up and looped. the track isnt released yet thank GOD but I wanna release it because the ting is FIRE and I already played it out at a set and people went CRAZY

Okay, good news that it's unreleased — you still have time to sort this out properly. Here's the situation:

**What you need to clear:**
For any sample, there are potentially TWO copyrights:
1. **The composition** (the song itself — melody, lyrics) — owned by the songwriter/publisher
2. **The master recording** (the actual recording) — owned by the label or artist

You need permission from BOTH owners to legally release a track with a sample.

**For a 2-second pitched vocal chop:**
This is a grey area. Some people will tell you "it's so short and so altered nobody will notice." That might be true practically, but legally there's no minimum length that makes sampling okay. Even 2 seconds can get you sued if someone recognizes it.

**Your options:**
1. Clear the sample properly (safest)
2. Replay/recreate the vocal yourself or with a singer (cheaper, no clearance needed)
3. Release it and hope nobody notices (risky, not recommended)

Do you know the specific track you sampled from?

yea I know the track. its from the 90s so the artist is still around but I have NO idea who owns the master. the label might not even exist anymore?? a lot of those old dancehall labels are gone still

That's actually a really common issue with 90s dancehall samples — the label landscape in Jamaica was chaotic back then. A lot of small labels didn't keep great records, some went under, rights got sold multiple times.

Here's how to track down the owners:

**For the composition (publishing):**
- Search **ASCAP, BMI, and SOCAN** databases online — they're free to search. Enter the song title and artist name. This will tell you who the publisher is.
- Also try **JASRAC** (Jamaica's performing rights org might have records)

**For the master (recording):**
- Check the original record/CD credits if you can find them
- **Discogs.com** — search the release and it usually lists the label
- Try reaching out to the artist directly on social media — many 90s dancehall artists are on Instagram now

**If you truly can't find the owner:**
This is where it gets tricky. You can't clear what you can't find. Some producers in this situation will:
- Document their search efforts ("good faith" attempt to clear)
- Release on smaller platforms first
- Set aside a percentage of royalties in case someone comes forward

But the safest route is always to find the owner. How important is this specific sample to the track?

its like THE HOOK of the whole track. the vocal chop IS the melody basically. I cant just take it out the whole song falls apart without it!! and I tried the SOCAN database before for a different ting and that website is PAINFUL to navigate

If it's the hook then you DEFINITELY need to clear it — that's not a subtle background element, that's the core of the song. If it ever blows up (which sounds possible since people went crazy at the set), the original rights holders WILL find out.

SOCAN's database is rough, I hear you. Try this order instead:

1. **BMI Repertoire Search** (repertoire.bmi.com) — cleanest interface
2. **ASCAP ACE Search** (ascap.com/repertory) — also pretty straightforward
3. **SOCAN** as a last resort

One of those three should have the publishing info if the songwriter registered the song properly.

For the master side — honestly, reaching out to the artist on Instagram or through their management might be your fastest route. Lots of 90s dancehall artists are approachable and some of them are happy to have their music sampled by a new generation. You might get a "yeah just give me a credit and a percentage" situation which is the best case.

The other option: **hire someone to replay the vocal.** If you get a singer to re-record that 2-second phrase in a similar style, you'd only need to clear the composition (publishing), not the master. That cuts your clearance work in half.
wait WHAT. if someone re-sings it I only need to clear ONE copyright instead of two?? thats actually a game changer. I know bare vocalists who could sing that part

Exactly! That's one of the most common workarounds in the industry. Here's why it works:

- The **master recording** copyright protects that SPECIFIC recording — the actual audio file, the performance captured on tape/digital
- The **composition** copyright protects the underlying song — the melody and lyrics

If you replay/re-sing the part, you're creating a NEW recording. So you don't need the master owner's permission because you're not using their recording. You only need the composition owner (publisher/songwriter) to approve a sample of their melody/lyrics.

Composition clearances are usually easier and cheaper to negotiate than master clearances. Sometimes it's just a percentage of royalties, no upfront fee.

So the move would be:
1. Find the publisher through BMI/ASCAP
2. Get a vocalist to re-sing the part
3. Negotiate a publishing split or clearance fee with the songwriter's publisher
4. Release the track legally

This is literally what major labels do all the time to avoid master clearance headaches.

yoooo okay THIS is the move. I'm gonna get my homegirl to re-sing it and just clear the publishing side. that seems way more manageable than trying to track down some ghost label from 1994 Kingston lol

Ha, yeah hunting down a 90s Jamaican label is a whole detective mission nobody asked for. The re-sing route is smart and clean.

Few tips for the re-recording:
- Get it as close to the original vibe as possible but it doesn't have to be identical — you're going to pitch and process it anyway
- Have your vocalist sign a simple session musician agreement so there's no confusion about who owns the new recording (you do)
- Keep the original sample in your project file as a reference but make sure the released version ONLY uses the re-sung version

And definitely still do the publisher search and get that composition clearance sorted before you drop the track. Don't skip that part.
one last ting — what does a composition clearance usually cost?? like am I looking at thousands of dollars or is it more reasonable??

It really varies, but for an independent release with a relatively unknown sample source, composition clearances can range from:

- **Royalty-only deal** — no upfront cost, you just give up a percentage of publishing (could be 15-50% depending on how much you used)
- **Small upfront fee + royalties** — maybe $500-2,000 plus a publishing split
- **Buyout** — one-time flat fee, but that's less common for indie releases

Since the original artist isn't a huge mainstream name, you're more likely to get a reasonable deal. The publisher might just want a credit and a percentage of publishing royalties. Start the negotiation low — offer 15-20% of the publishing — and see where they land.

If they come back with something crazy, that's when you weigh whether to pay it or find a completely different sample.

bless bless BLESS!! okay that's actually not as scary as I thought. you just saved me so much stress and probably a lawsuit lol. I'm hitting up BMI tonight and calling my girl tomorrow about the vocal. this track is TOO good to let it die over copyright wahala!!

Glad I could help! That track deserves to come out properly — especially if it's already getting reactions when you play it live. Get the publisher info, get the re-sing done, and you'll be releasing it stress-free.

Good luck with the clearance and the recording. Let the track live!
